lessons.title: Learn: Linux Permissions and Ownership
lessons.content_md:
# Lab 04: Permissions and Ownership

In Linux, permissions control who can read, write, or execute files. Ownership defines which user and group are responsible for a file.

## Learning Objectives

- Read and understand permission strings from `ls -l`
- Change permissions using `chmod`
- Understand owner/group concepts and `chown` syntax
- Apply secure defaults for scripts and sensitive files

## Core Topics

### 1) Permission Model

Each file has three permission sets:

- user (owner)
- group
- others

Example:

```
-rw-r--r--
```

- owner: read + write
- group: read
- others: read

### 2) Numeric Permission Values

- read = 4
- write = 2
- execute = 1

Common modes:

- 600: private file (owner rw only)
- 644: normal text file
- 700: private executable directory
- 755: executable script for everyone

### 3) Ownership

Use `ls -l` to inspect owner/group fields.

Use `chown` syntax:

```bash
chown user file
chown user:group file
```

### 4) Security Best Practices

- Never use 777 for sensitive files
- Use 600 for secret files
- Use 700 for private script directories
- Check effective permissions after every change

After reading, complete command practice, then exercises, then MCQ.

lessons.title: Command Practice Part 1 (10 Commands)
lessons.content_md:
## Guided Commands: Part 1

Create files and inspect default permissions.

```bash
cd ~ && pwd
mkdir -p ~/lab4/private ~/lab4/public
touch ~/lab4/private/secret.txt
echo 'top secret' > ~/lab4/private/secret.txt
ls -l ~/lab4/private/secret.txt
chmod 600 ~/lab4/private/secret.txt
ls -l ~/lab4/private/secret.txt
touch ~/lab4/public/readme.txt
chmod 644 ~/lab4/public/readme.txt
ls -l ~/lab4/public/readme.txt
```

tasks.description: C01: Go to home and print current directory.
C02: Create lab4 with private and public directories.
C03: Create secret.txt in private directory.
C04: Write top secret into secret.txt.
C05: Show permissions of secret.txt before chmod.
C06: Set secret.txt permission to 600.
C07: Verify permission 600 on secret.txt.
C08: Create readme.txt in public directory.
C09: Set readme.txt permission to 644.
C10: Verify permission on readme.txt.

lessons.title: Command Practice Part 2 (10 Commands)
lessons.content_md:
## Guided Commands: Part 2

Practice execute permissions and ownership inspection.

```bash
printf '#!/bin/bash
echo hello
' > ~/lab4/public/hello.sh
ls -l ~/lab4/public/hello.sh
chmod 755 ~/lab4/public/hello.sh
ls -l ~/lab4/public/hello.sh
~/lab4/public/hello.sh
chmod 700 ~/lab4/private
ls -ld ~/lab4/private
ls -l ~/lab4
id
ls -l ~/lab4/private/secret.txt
```

tasks.description: C11: Create hello.sh script with simple echo command.
C12: Check current permissions of hello.sh.
C13: Set hello.sh permission to 755.
C14: Verify 755 permission on hello.sh.
C15: Execute hello.sh script.
C16: Set private directory permission to 700.
C17: Verify directory permission for private folder.
C18: List all files under lab4 with permission details.
C19: Show your user and group IDs.
C20: Check owner and group of secret.txt.

lessons.title: Exercises (5)
lessons.content_md:
## Practical Exercises

Complete all permission and ownership exercises.

- E1-E2: easy
- E3-E4: medium
- E5: medium-hard

tasks.description: E1: Create credentials.txt in ~/lab4/private with content 'token=abc123'.
E2: Set credentials.txt permission to 600 and verify.
E3: Create deploy.sh in public, make it executable (755), and confirm permission.
tasks.hint:
Type: printf '#!/bin/bash
echo deploy
' > ~/lab4/public/deploy.sh && chmod 755 ~/lab4/public/deploy.sh && ls -l ~/lab4/public/deploy.sh

tasks.description: E4: Set public directory permission to 755 and verify.
E5: List private directory details and verify owner with id command output.

lessons.title: MCQ Quiz (5)
lessons.content_md:
## MCQ: Permissions and Ownership

Answer with option index (0-3). Example: submit 2.

tasks.description: Q1: Which permission mode gives owner read/write only?
Q2: Which command changes file permissions?
Q3: What does 755 mean for a script?
tasks.validation_rule: ["owner full, group/others read+execute","owner read only","everyone write only","no one execute"]

tasks.description: Q4: Which command shows user and group IDs?
Q5: Which setting is safest for private secrets file?
